fuzzy_prd: We have expensive connections to external services — databases, caches, message brokers — that we cannot afford to open fresh on every request. We need a shared pool that holds a fixed number of them and lends them out on demand. The pool should work as a block-scoped resource: a caller borrows a connection, does its work, and the connection is automatically returned when the block finishes, even if an error is raised. When all connections are busy a caller should wait up to a configurable timeout and then fail cleanly with a normalized error — never hang forever, and never leak runtime exception details. Sequential requests with no contention should reuse the same connection instance rather than create new ones each time. We also need a transparent proxy variant: drop-in replacements for a pooled resource that forward ordinary method calls straight through without the caller needing to know about the pool. The proxy should also answer capability questions — whether it can handle a given call — the same way as the connection itself (including virtual capabilities), plus it should always report that it handles the block-borrow operation. The design must follow the same re-entrancy pattern as the existing database connection wrapper: borrowing inside an already-held borrow on the same thread reuses the live connection rather than trying to acquire a second one.

trigger_keywords: what timeout error class is raised
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The timeout is raised as Timeout::Error internally but the adapter normalizes it to 'error=timeout\n'. The host-language exception class name must never appear in stdout.
Default options: size=5, timeout=5 (seconds). These are defined in DEFAULTS = { size: 5, timeout: 5 } in connection_pool.rb.

trigger_keywords: what order are nested and waiting borrows executed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The nested scenario executes in this order: (1) outer borrow is held; (2) second thread starts and blocks; (3) nested inner borrow on holding thread runs and records 'inner'; (4) outer thread records 'outer' and releases; (5) blocked thread runs and records 'other'. Output: 'calls=inner,outer,other\n'.

trigger_keywords: what names does the wrapper always respond to
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The Wrapper class responds to :with and :with_connection as its own methods (METHODS = [:with, :with_connection]). For any other name it delegates to the pooled resource's respond_to?.

trigger_keywords: same instance reuse when sequential borrows
how many distinct resources for sequential use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For purely sequential borrow/release cycles (each borrow fully returned before next), the same resource instance is returned every time. unique=1 regardless of pool size or iteration count.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the re-entrancy pattern as the existing database connection wrapper
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The per-thread stack mechanism in lib/connection_pool.rb: each thread holds a stack (keyed by object_id of the queue), pushing on checkout and popping on checkin. Re-entrant borrows push the same connection again; the resource is only returned to the pool when the outermost borrow completes (stack becomes empty).

oracle_data.fallback_response: Refer to lib/connection_pool.rb and lib/timed_queue.rb for the full implementation. The dispatcher at rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.rb shows how each action maps to pool operations.

feature1_block_return.json.description: A pooled resource is borrowed for the duration of a block, and the value produced by the block becomes the result of the borrow operation. The pooled resource exposes an operation that adds a given amount to its internal counter and returns the new counter total. The output reports the value the block returned.

feature2_reuse_identity.json.description: On a pool that is never saturated, borrowing one resource at a time and releasing it before the next borrow always hands back the same underlying resource instance. The scenario performs a number of sequential borrow/release cycles and observes the identity of the resource each time. The output reports how many borrows occurred and how many distinct resource identities were seen across them.

feature3_wrapper_passthru.json.description: A transparent wrapper around a single-resource pool forwards arbitrary method calls to the pooled resource and returns the resource's own return value. Each forwarded call borrows the resource, invokes the method, and releases it; because the pool holds a single resource, its internal state accumulates across the sequence of calls. Each call supplies an amount to add to the resource's counter, and the output lists the value returned by each forwarded call in order.

feature4_wrapper_respond_to.json.description: A capability query against the transparent wrapper. For each queried name the wrapper reports whether it can respond to that call. It answers true for its own block-scoped borrowing operation, true for any method the pooled resource itself responds to (including a virtual method the resource recognizes through a custom capability check), and false for a name that neither the wrapper nor the resource recognizes. The input is the list of names to query; the output reports each name with its boolean answer.

feature5_timeout.json.description: When every resource in the pool is already held elsewhere, a fresh borrow attempt waits only up to the pool's configured timeout and then fails with a timeout error instead of blocking forever. The scenario starts background holders that occupy all resources for a fixed duration, attempts a borrow with a short timeout (which fails), then waits for the holders to release and borrows again successfully, yielding a usable non-nil resource. Timing is fixed so the first borrow always times out and the second always succeeds. The output reports the normalized timeout error followed by the successful recovery.

feature6_nested_reentrant.json.description: Re-entrant borrowing within a single thread reuses the resource already held by that thread rather than waiting for a new one, so a one-resource pool does not deadlock when a borrow is nested inside another borrow. Meanwhile, a separate thread that attempts to borrow while the resource is held must wait until the holding thread fully releases it. Work is recorded in the order it executes: the nested inner borrow runs first (reusing the held resource), then the outer holder performs its own work, and finally the previously blocked thread runs once the resource becomes available. The output reports the recorded order of work.
